Run 'service tomcat8 restart' to restart the tomcat daemon.
Run 'service casserver restart' to restart the casserver daemon.
Continue performing other operations in the CVM system immediately after issuing the restart command, since the CVM remains fully available.
explanation: The CVM system becomes unavailable while the restart command is executing; all tasks must be stopped beforehand, and no operations should be performed during the procedure.

question: Regarding CAS log files located in the CVK /var directory, which descriptions are INCORRECT? (Select all that apply.)
options: Ocfs2_shell_XX.log is the script log of the shared file system.
The HA log is self-developed and is included in the CVK /var directory.
The vSwitch log and the 'log of commands manually executed in the background' are both listed entries in the CVK /var directory.
The CVK /var directory contains no VM-related log files.
The fsm storage-manager log is excluded from the CVK /var directory.
explanation: The CVK /var directory contains HA logs (self-developed), Ocfs2_shell_XX.log, VM-related logs, vSwitch logs, the log of commands manually executed in the background, and the fsm storage-manager log. Statements 4 and 5 contradict this.

question: Regarding the CAS performance-optimization configuration ideas, which statements are INCORRECT? (Select all that apply.)
options: HugePages should be enabled on the host first, then enabled on the VM.
Disk Provision should choose 'Lazy Zeroed' as the recommended performance-optimization option.
Disk Provision should choose 'Eager Zeroed' as the recommended performance-optimization option.
HugePages must be enabled on the VM first; the host-side configuration is unnecessary.
'Configure I/O Priority' and 'Configure CPU Model' are listed performance-optimization items.
explanation: Disk Provision should choose 'Lazy Zeroed' (not Eager Zeroed), and HugePages must be enabled on the host first and then on the VM. The other statements match the recommended optimization items.

question: Which command is used to query the disk structure of a VM in the background of a CVK host?
options: virsh pool-list --all
virsh domblklist ID
virsh list --all
qemu-img info <VM image file>
explanation: 'virsh domblklist ID' queries the VM disk structure in the background of the CVK host. 'virsh pool-list --all' queries storage pools, 'virsh list --all' queries VMs, and 'qemu-img info' queries image-file information.

question: Which command is used to query the storage-pool status in the background of a CVK host?
options: virsh pool-status
virsh pool-list --all
virsh storage-list
Ovs-vsctl pool-show
explanation: 'virsh pool-list --all' lists storage pools in the background of the CVK host, returning Name/State/Autostart columns for entries such as defaultpool, isopool, and ONEStor_High_LUN01.

question: Which command is used to restart the libvirtd service on a CVK host?
options: Service libvirt-restart
Systemctl libvirtd reload
Service libvirtd restart
virsh libvirtd-restart
explanation: 'service libvirtd restart' is the maintenance command to restart the libvirtd daemon on the CVK host (alongside 'service casserver restart' for the CAS server).

question: Which qemu-img command is used to combine VM image files into a single qcow2 file?
options: qemu-img info <VM image file>
qemu-img merge -f qcow2 xxx
qemu-img convert -O qcow2 -f qcow2 xxx
qemu-img combine -O qcow2 xxx
explanation: 'qemu-img convert -O qcow2 -f qcow2 xxx' combines image files into a single qcow2 file. 'qemu-img info' queries image information; the other forms are not valid qemu-img subcommands.

question: Which Linux command among the CAS common maintenance commands is used to capture packets on a CVK host?
options: Ifconfig
Tcpdump
Iostat
Uptime
explanation: 'tcpdump' captures packets. 'ifconfig' queries NIC information, 'iostat' queries device load, and 'uptime' queries system start time and load.

question: Which key on the keyboard is used in vi/vim to switch from edit mode back to common mode?
options: Ctrl+C
Tab
ESC
:q
explanation: Pressing ESC switches vi/vim from edit mode back to common mode. ':wq' saves and exits, ':q' only quits, and Ctrl+C and Tab are not mode-switch keys for vi/vim.

question: In iostat output, what does the column header 'TPS' represent?
options: Total physical sectors written by the device.
The number of I/O read and write requests issued to the device per second.
The number of TCP packets sent per second.
Total partitions per disk device.
explanation: In iostat, TPS is the number of I/O read and write requests issued to the device per second. The other definitions describe kB_read/kB_wrtn or are not iostat metrics.

question: Which Linux command in the CAS common maintenance command set is used to copy files remotely between hosts?
options: Cp
Scp
Mv
Tar
explanation: 'scp' copies files remotely between hosts. 'cp' is the local copy, 'mv' moves or renames files, and 'tar' compresses and packs files.

question: Which of the following are valid options for the upgrade.sh script used in CAS version upgrade?
options: -p cvks
Precheck
Postcheck
--force-CVK
--rollback-CVM
explanation: The standard usage is './upgrade.sh [option]', where option is one of: -p cvks | precheck | postcheck | copy | -f CVM | -f CVK | --force-CVK | restore | mysql. '--rollback-CVM' is not a valid option.

question: Which steps are part of the standard procedure for replacing a network interface card (NIC) on a CVK host?
options: Back up the NIC configuration files.
Migrate the VMs and back up management-platform data.
Enter maintenance mode and shut down the host.
After the replacement, check cluster status and perform one-key inspection.
Skip the cluster-health pre-check to accelerate the swap.
explanation: The NIC replacement workflow is: check cluster health, back up NIC configuration files, migrate VMs, back up management-platform data, enter maintenance mode, shut down the host, replace the NIC, exit maintenance mode, check cluster status, and run one-key inspection.

question: Which statements about NIC replacement and the resulting configuration files on a CVK host are correct?
options: On Ubuntu, the NIC configuration file is /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules.
On CentOS, the NIC configuration file is located at /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-ethx.
After a NIC replacement on Ubuntu, the eth number of the new NIC is appended after the original entry, and the MAC must be updated to restore networking.
On CentOS, no new ifcfg file is created after a NIC replacement; only the existing file's MAC is rewritten automatically.
Backing up the NIC configuration files is unnecessary before NIC replacement on either OS.
explanation: Ubuntu uses /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules and CentOS uses /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-ethx. After replacement on Ubuntu the new eth number is appended after the original entry and the MAC must be updated. On CentOS a new ifcfg-ethx file is added, and NIC configuration files must be backed up.

question: Which statements about the stateful-failover (CVM HA) cmsd service procedures are correct?
options: Modifying the virtual IP address requires stopping the cmsd service, modifying the IP, and starting the cmsd service.
When adding a new CVK node as an advanced host, copy the /etc/cmsd folder from the existing node to the new node.
On Ubuntu, 'service cron restart' is run after appending the ms_cmsd_realtime_check.sh crontab entry.
On H3Linux, the cmsd service is enabled with 'systemctl enable cmsd.service' so it starts automatically.
Modifying the virtual IP address requires destroying and reinstalling the entire cmsd configuration package.
explanation: The virtual-IP modification procedure is Check status, Stop cmsd, Check, Modify IP, Start cmsd, Check. Copying /etc/cmsd to a new CVK node, running 'service cron restart' on Ubuntu after the crontab edit, and 'systemctl enable cmsd.service' on H3Linux are all valid. A destructive reinstall is not part of the procedure.

question: Which items are part of the CAS performance-optimization configuration ideas?
options: Configure I/O Priority.
Configure CPU Model.
Configure HugePages (enable on the host first, then on the VM).
Disk Provision chooses Lazy Zeroed.
Enable Memory Ballooning by default on every VM.
explanation: Performance optimization comprises four items: Configure I/O Priority, Configure CPU Model, Configure HugePages (host first, then VM), and Disk Provision Lazy Zeroed. Memory Ballooning is not part of the optimization list.

question: Which resolution steps are recommended for a failed Windows VM whose console does not respond to keyboard input (after Ctrl+Alt+Del)?
options: If the Windows disk is corrupt, mount an.iso repair image file onto the VM.
Configure the VM to start from the CD/DVD drive, then restart the VM and open its console.
Press any key at the boot prompt in the console to start from the CD/DVD for repair or reinstallation.
If no guest OS is installed, mount the guest OS.iso or configure the VM to obtain the guest OS from a remote server over the network.
Force-delete the VM and recreate it from scratch with a different hypervisor.
explanation: The recommended steps are: mount the.iso repair image, set the CD/DVD as the boot device, press any key at the boot prompt, and mount the guest OS.iso or fetch the OS over the network. Deleting the VM or switching hypervisor is not recommended.

question: Regarding standard qemu-img and virsh commands on a CVK host, which statement is INCORRECT?
options: 'qemu-img info <VM image file>' queries VM image file information.
'virsh domblklist ID' queries the VM disk structure in the background of the CVK host.
'virsh pool-list --all' queries the storage-pool status in the background.
'virsh image-merge' is the standard command to combine multiple qcow2 image files into one.
explanation: The standard command to combine qcow2 image files is 'qemu-img convert -O qcow2 -f qcow2 xxx'. 'virsh image-merge' is not a valid command.

question: Concerning troubleshooting a garbled CVM webpage when logging in to CVM, which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Accessing one system from multiple browser windows on one computer can cause data exceptions.
Resizing browser windows several times in a short interval can cause garbled CVM webpages.
A browser containing buffered webpage contents is a known condition leading to the garbled webpage.
The recommended resolution is to open multiple tabs and use a different browser for each, rather than clearing the browser buffer.
explanation: The recommended resolution is the opposite: open only one tab in only one browser window per computer to access CVM, and clear the browser buffer before logging in again. Multiple tabs and multiple browsers are causes of the issue, not the fix.

question: Regarding the procedure to modify the IP address and host name of a CVK host, which description is INCORRECT?
options: Migrate the VMs off the host before modifying.
Delete the host from the cluster, then modify the IP and host name.
Add the host again, then migrate the VMs back to the host.
It is sufficient to edit the hosts file via SSH while the host remains in the cluster, without migrating any VMs.
explanation: The correct process is: migrate the VMs, delete the host from the cluster, modify the IP and host name, add the host again, and migrate the VMs back. Editing in place while the host stays in the cluster is not the standard procedure.

question: Regarding troubleshooting for the 'Disabling IRQ #10 BUG: soft lockup - CPU#0 stuck for 67s! [migration/0:5]' error when shutting down Linux VMs on CAS, which statements are INCORRECT? (Select all that apply.)
options: The error typically occurs when a VM uses a virtio NIC with poor compatibility on some Linux versions.
When this error message appears, the VM shutdown procedure is almost finished, and the VM can be powered off from CAS to force shutdown without incurring problems.
The recommended solution is to immediately downgrade the host's libvirtd to a previous version and reboot the entire cluster.
The recommended solution is to replace the virtio NIC with an e1000 NIC in production before any attempt to power off the VM.
Power-off from CAS is treated as a safe forced-shutdown action in this scenario.
explanation: The recommended solution is to power off the VM from CAS to force shutdown without incurring problems. Downgrading libvirtd or swapping the NIC type are not recommended. The other statements match the standard condition and resolution.

question: Concerning the resolution of 'failure to format the disk of a shared file system' and 'failure to set up the network with CAStools after deploying a Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 VM', which statements are INCORRECT? (Select all that apply.)
options: Disk formatting of a shared file system is available only when a storage pool has been specified for at least one host and the pool is not active.

If formatting fails because the pool is not attached, add a host pool that uses the shared file system to the host and suspend the storage pool, then reformat from the host-pool / shared-file-system tab.
For the RHEL 7 CAStools network-setup failure, install the most recent version of CAStools on the VM, run 'chkconfig NetworkManager off' to disable NetworkManager, then reconfigure the template.
The shared-file-system disk can be formatted while the storage pool is active and currently mounted by running VMs.
The RHEL 7 CAStools issue is resolved by enabling NetworkManager via 'chkconfig NetworkManager on' and rebooting the host.
explanation: The storage pool must NOT be active during reformat, and the RHEL 7 fix is to DISABLE NetworkManager ('chkconfig NetworkManager off') after updating CAStools, not enable it. The first three statements match the recommended resolutions.
